[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Mohammed+V+International+Airport%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Mohammed+V+International+Airport%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Mohammed+V+International+Airport%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Mohammed+V+International+Airport%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(February 2012)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

is an [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") serving [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"). Located in [Nouaceur Province](/wiki/Nouaceur_Province "Nouaceur Province"), it is operated by [Airports of Morocco](/wiki/Airports_of_Morocco "Airports of Morocco").

and it served as an auxiliary airfield for Casablanca's [Anfa Airport](/wiki/Anfa_Airport "Anfa Airport").[[6]](#cite_note-7) The airfield handled diverse military traffic as a stopover en route to [Port Lyautey Airfield](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Port_Lyautey "Naval Air Station Port Lyautey"), and to [Marrakesh Menara Airport](/wiki/Marrakesh_Menara_Airport "Marrakesh Menara Airport") on the North African [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo")-[Dakar](/wiki/Dakar "Dakar") route. In addition, it was the terminus of Mid-Atlantic route transatlantic flights via the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") to [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") and airfields on the [East Coast of the United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States").

and was used as a [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") [Strategic Air Command](/wiki/Strategic_Air_Command "Strategic Air Command") staging area for [B-47 Stratojet](/wiki/B-47_Stratojet "B-47 Stratojet") bombers pointed at the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). These operations later moved to [Ben Guerir Air Base](/wiki/Ben_Guerir_Air_Base "Ben Guerir Air Base").

**Mohammed V International Airport**[[a]](#cite_note-3) [IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): **CMN**, [ICAO](/wiki/ICAO_airport_code "ICAO airport code"): **GMMN** is an [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") serving [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"). Located in [Nouaceur Province](/wiki/Nouaceur_Province "Nouaceur Province"), it is operated by [Airports of Morocco](/wiki/Airports_of_Morocco "Airports of Morocco").

With about 7.6 million passengers passing through the airport in 2022, it was the busiest airport in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") and in the top 10 of [busiest airports in Africa](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_in_Africa "List of busiest airports in Africa").[[3]](#cite_note-4) Passenger traffic in 2022 had recovered to 74% of the total [pre-pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Morocco "COVID-19 pandemic in Morocco") numbers of 2019.[[4]](#cite_note-5) The airport serves as hub for [Royal Air Maroc](/wiki/Royal_Air_Maroc "Royal Air Maroc"),[[5]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_sees_fleet,_hub_growth_ahead_of_oneworld_membership-6) [Royal Air Maroc Express](/wiki/Royal_Air_Maroc_Express "Royal Air Maroc Express") and [Air Arabia Maroc](/wiki/Air_Arabia_Maroc "Air Arabia Maroc"). The airport is named after King [Mohammed V of Morocco](/wiki/Mohammed_V_of_Morocco "Mohammed V of Morocco").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1940s")]

The Casablanca Mohammed V Airport was originally built by the United States in early 1943 following [Operation Torch](/wiki/Operation_Torch#Casablanca "Operation Torch") in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It was named **Berrechid Airfield** and it served as an auxiliary airfield for Casablanca's [Anfa Airport](/wiki/Anfa_Airport "Anfa Airport").[[6]](#cite_note-7) The airfield handled diverse military traffic as a stopover en route to [Port Lyautey Airfield](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Port_Lyautey "Naval Air Station Port Lyautey"), and to [Marrakesh Menara Airport](/wiki/Marrakesh_Menara_Airport "Marrakesh Menara Airport") on the North African [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo")-[Dakar](/wiki/Dakar "Dakar") route. In addition, it was the terminus of Mid-Atlantic route transatlantic flights via the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") to [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") and airfields on the [East Coast of the United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States").

In addition to its transportation role, the airfield supported the [North African Campaign](/wiki/North_African_Campaign "North African Campaign") with the [Twelfth Air Force](/wiki/Twelfth_Air_Force "Twelfth Air Force") [68th Reconnaissance Group](/wiki/68th_Reconnaissance_Group "68th Reconnaissance Group") operating photo-reconnaissance versions of the [P-38 Lightning](/wiki/P-38_Lightning "P-38 Lightning") and [P-51 Mustang](/wiki/P-51_Mustang "P-51 Mustang"). Part of the 68th first arrived at [Angads Airport](/wiki/Angads_Airport "Angads Airport") in [Oujda](/wiki/Oujda "Oujda") in November 1942 and moved to Berrechid in March 1943 upon its completion. It flew both antisubmarine missions over the Atlantic and photo-reconnaissance combat missions over German-held territory until early September when it moved east to [Massicault Airfield](/wiki/Massicault_Airfield "Massicault Airfield") in Tunisia. With the end of the war in 1945, the airfield was handed over to the civil government.

### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1950s")]

During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") in the early and middle 1950s, the airfield was reopened as **Nouasseur Air Base** and was used as a [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") [Strategic Air Command](/wiki/Strategic_Air_Command "Strategic Air Command") staging area for [B-47 Stratojet](/wiki/B-47_Stratojet "B-47 Stratojet") bombers pointed at the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). These operations later moved to [Ben Guerir Air Base](/wiki/Ben_Guerir_Air_Base "Ben Guerir Air Base").

With the destabilisation of French government in Morocco, and [Moroccan independence](/wiki/History_of_Morocco "History of Morocco") in 1956, the government of [Mohammed V](/wiki/Mohammed_V_of_Morocco "Mohammed V of Morocco") wanted the US Air Force to pull its bases out of Morocco, insisting on such action after American intervention in Lebanon in 1958. The United States agreed to leave in December 1959, and was fully out of Morocco by 1963. The U.S. felt that, with the long range of the [B-52](/wiki/Boeing_B-52_Stratofortress "Boeing B-52 Stratofortress") and completion of Spanish bases in 1959, the Moroccan bases were no longer important.

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2020s")]

In 2022, Mohammed V Airport was ranked the 4th busiest airport in Africa.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

#### New terminal project (2025–2029)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New terminal project (2025–2029)")]

In May 2025 Morocco's airports authority [Moroccan Airports Authority](/wiki/Moroccan_Airports_Authority "Moroccan Airports Authority") launched an international tender to build a new H-shaped terminal at Mohammed V International Airport in Casablanca at an estimated cost of US$1.6 billion.[[8]](#cite_note-Eljechtimi-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10) The terminal, designed in a “hub-style”, will increase capacity from roughly 15 million to 35 million passengers per year. It will form part of Morocco's Airports 2030 strategy and is expected to be completed by 2029 in time for the 2030 FIFA World Cup which Morocco will co-host with Spain and Portugal.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The expansion project features:

* A new 3,700 m parallel runway, extended taxiways and a new 42 m air traffic control tower.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
* An integrated high-speed rail (LGV) station linking the airport with Casablanca, Rabat and Marrakesh via the Al Boraq line.[[8]](#cite_note-Eljechtimi-9)

Earthworks began in mid-2025 with main construction slated to follow and full operation targeted for 2029.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Ground transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ground transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Train_Station_in_CMN_Airport.jpg/250px-Train_Station_in_CMN_Airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Train_Station_in_CMN_Airport.jpg)

The train station in Casablanca Mohammed V Airport

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rail")]

The [Al Bidaoui](/wiki/Al_Bidaoui "Al Bidaoui") train service, operated by [ONCF](/wiki/ONCF "ONCF") from 04:00 to 23:00, is available every hour and connects the airport to Casablanca's two main railway stations, [Casa-Port Railway Terminal](/wiki/Casa-Port_Railway_Terminal "Casa-Port Railway Terminal") and [Casa-Voyageurs Railway Station](/wiki/Casa-Voyageurs_Railway_Station "Casa-Voyageurs Railway Station").[[85]](#cite_note-86)

### Car

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Car")]

* From Casablanca main access is by [A3 Motorway](/wiki/Casablanca%E2%80%93Agadir_expressway "Casablanca–Agadir expressway");
* From Rabat by [A1 Motorway](/wiki/Casablanca%E2%80%93Rabat_expressway "Casablanca–Rabat expressway") through Tit Mellil and [A31 Motorway](/w/index.php?title=Tit_mellil_-_Berrechid_Motorway&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tit mellil - Berrechid Motorway (page does not exist)");
* From Beni Mellal by [A4 Motorway](/wiki/Berrechid%E2%80%93Beni_Mellal_expressway "Berrechid–Beni Mellal expressway");
* From Marrakesh by [A3 Motorway](/wiki/Casablanca%E2%80%93Agadir_expressway "Casablanca–Agadir expressway") exit km 225;
* From El Jadida by [A1 Motorway](/wiki/Casablanca%E2%80%93Safi_expressway "Casablanca–Safi expressway") and [A3 Motorway](/wiki/Casablanca%E2%80%93Agadir_expressway "Casablanca–Agadir expressway").[[86]](#cite_note-87)

## Incidents and accidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mohammed_V_International_Airport&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Incidents and accidents")]

* On 1 April 1970, a [Royal Air Maroc](/wiki/Royal_Air_Maroc "Royal Air Maroc") [Sud Aviation SE-210 Caravelle](/wiki/Sud_Aviation_Caravelle "Sud Aviation Caravelle") crashed on approach to Casablanca Mohammed V airport when it lost control at a height of about 500 feet. The fuselage broke in two. Sixty-one of the eighty-two passengers and crew were killed.[[87]](#cite_note-airsafe-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)
